summary: This initiative delivers a habit-tracking web application that helps users build consistency through customizable reminders and progress visualization. Users select one messaging channel (SMS, Telegram, or WhatsApp) at signup and receive reminders they can respond to directly via messaging to mark habits complete. The system tracks streaks, displays progress charts, and enforces a 5-habit limit per user with timezone-aware scheduling.

assumptions.text: We assume users will accept permanent messaging channel selection at signup with no ability to switch channels later
We assume best-effort reminder delivery without retry mechanisms is acceptable for POC scope, meaning failed reminders are permanently lost
We assume users will understand and correctly use the 'Habit (xxxx): done' reply format without extensive onboarding
We assume the inability to edit habits or restart stopped habits is acceptable for POC, requiring users to delete and recreate instead
We assume verification code authentication via messaging channels provides sufficient security without password-based authentication

requirementsInterpreted.text: Users can create up to 5 custom daily habits with flexible reminder schedules including daily, every 2 days, weekly, monthly, hourly intervals (1h, 6h), or custom time-of-day schedules (up to 12 times per day)
Users select one messaging channel (SMS, Telegram, or WhatsApp) during signup and receive all reminders through that channel only, with no ability to change channels later
Users can mark habits complete by replying to reminder messages with format 'Habit (xxxx): done' or via web interface, receiving confirmation message '[Habit Name] Completed!'
System authenticates users via 6-digit verification codes sent to their selected messaging channel during signup, with no password-based authentication
System displays progress charts showing habit completion over time and tracks current streak counts, with charts loading within 2 seconds
All reminders are scheduled according to user's selected timezone (chosen at signup), and habits can only be marked complete for the current day in that timezone
System processes bi-directional messages within 5 seconds and ignores replies to outdated reminder messages (only accepting replies to the most recent reminder per habit)
When users delete or stop a habit, all pending reminders are immediately cancelled; stopped habits cannot be restarted and must be recreated
System prevents duplicate completions of the same habit on the same day and prevents marking habits complete for past or future dates
Habits cannot be edited after creation; users must delete and recreate to make changes

outOfScope.text: Social features, habit sharing between users, or community functionality
Advanced analytics, AI-powered recommendations, or predictive habit insights
Multi-language support beyond English for messages and user interface
Offline mode, local data caching, or functionality without active internet connection
Reminder delivery retry mechanisms or failure handling (POC accepts silent failures)

openQuestions.text: What is the expected format and character set for habit identifiers (xxxx) in the reply format 'Habit (xxxx): done'?
How long should verification codes remain valid before expiration, and how many incorrect verification attempts should be allowed?
What time window defines when a reminder message becomes 'outdated' and replies should be ignored?
Should the system provide any user feedback when reminder delivery fails, or is silent failure acceptable for POC?

riskFlags.text: High dependency on external messaging APIs (SMS, Telegram, WhatsApp) with potential rate limits, costs, and reliability issues that could block core functionality
No stakeholders, timeline, or budget identified in the brief, creating uncertainty around approval, resources, and delivery expectations
Bi-directional messaging adds significant complexity through webhook infrastructure, message parsing, and race conditions between habit deletion and reminder delivery
POC scope with no retry mechanisms means failed reminders are permanently lost, potentially causing poor user experience and low retention
